data.title: Cathie Wood's ARK Dumps $26 Million Palantir Shares, Bulks Up on Airbnb
data.description:
May 16 - Cathie Wood's ARK (ARKK) ETFs shifted their bets in the latest trading session. ARK's flagship ARKK ETF snapped up 214,624 shares of Airbnb Inc , spending about $29.6 million on the home-rental platform. Its ARKF ETF added 140,000 shares of eToro Group Ltd for roughly $7.3 million and scooped up 31,983 shares of CRISPR Therapeutics AG at a cost of about $1.2 million.

On the sell side, ARKK trimmed 206,881 shares of Palantir Technologies Inc , unloading approximately $26.5 million worth of the data-analytics firm. ARKF also pared back UiPath Inc by 132,149 shares, netting around $1.8 million.

ARK's innovation-focused ARKW ETF topped up its GitLab Inc position with 22,471 shares for $1.2 million. The ETFs made smaller moves in Blade Air Mobility Inc , Nextdoor Holdings Inc and Twist Bioscience Corp , reflecting ARK's strategy to balance high-conviction bets with a broad tech and biotech lineup.

These adjustments underscore ARK's willingness to rotate capital toward companies it views as poised for growth, like ABNB and CRSP, while reducing exposure to names where sentiment may have peaked.

data.title: Upwork Is Maintained at Market Outperform by JMP Securities
Ukraine tells investors it won't rush $3.2 bln GDP-linked debt restructuring
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ukraine owes over $500 million to warrantholders in June
  • Butsa emphasizes no rush for warrant restructuring, prioritizes right deal
  • Warrantholder committee, led by hedge funds, opposes full restructuring
  • Ukraine's economy rebounded sharply post-2022 invasion, affecting warrant payouts

Ukraine's chief debt negotiator told investors in London this week that Kyiv is in no hurry to restructure its GDP-linked debt, three sources told Reuters, effectively preparing them for possible default on a payment due within weeks.

The meetings in London between investors and Ukraine's debt chief, Yuriy Butsa, took place as senior Russian and Ukrainian negotiators met in Turkey for the first direct talks over the war in more than three years.

Ukraine is supposed to make a more than half a billion dollar payment on the so-called 'GDP warrants' on which it owes $3.2 billion by early June after restructuring talks with a core group of their holders broke down last month.

According to sources, Butsa told investors that Ukraine would take its time to get the right deal rather than rushing to strike an agreement before the upcoming deadline.

"They've made it clear there is a moratorium in place and ... there is no cross default to the bonds," one investor told Reuters.

There was no dedicated meeting with the warrantholder group, one source said.

Ukraine's debt office told Reuters it had been, "actively engaging with stakeholders and gathering market feedback to identify the best path forward for the treatment of the warrants," as part of efforts to make its debt sustainable.

The sources said Butsa underlined that a near-term solution to rework the warrants is a priority, but that they were not afraid to miss the payment.

The message is in line with what Kyiv has told the market for weeks. Ukraine's bonds traded mixed and warrants flat on Friday with earlier gains fuelled by optimism around the talks in Istanbul fading.

Ukraine threw in the GDP warrants - fixed income securities linked to economic growth - to sweeten its 2015 debt restructuring following Russia's annexation of Crimea.

But their complex structure meant they were not part of last year's broader $20 billion restructuring that became necessary following Moscow's full-scale invasion in 2022.

Ukrainian officials have said the near-30% collapse in its economy after Russia's 2022 invasion means that data showing it clawing back from that brink - rather than resounding economic strength - can now trigger the payments.

Finance minister Serhii Marchenko described them last month as being "designed for a world that no longer exists".

The warrantholder committee, which sources said is led by prominent hedge funds, has said a full restructuring of the warrants is "neither appropriate nor necessary" given the previous concessions made.

data.title: Buffett's Berkshire Sells More Bank Shares, Doubles Down on Constellation
data.description:
May 16 - Warren Buffett (Trades, Portfolio)'s Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) trimmed its bank holdings and ramped up its stake in Constellation Brands in the first quarter, according to a filing with the SEC.

The conglomerate exited its remaining 14.6 million shares of Citigroup and 40.2 million shares of Nubank . It also pared back Bank of America to 631.6 million shares from 680.2 million, cut Capital One Financial (COF) to 7.15 million shares from 7.45 million, and trimmed DaVita to 35.1 million from 36.1 million.

On the buy side, Berkshire nearly doubled its Constellation Brands position to 12 million shares, valued at $2.2 billion on March 31. It lifted its VeriSign stake to 13.3 million shares, worth $3.37 billion, and boosted Pool Corp. to 1.46 million shares from 0.6 million.

Notably, the firm left its Apple holding unchanged at 300 million shares, valued at $66.6 billion. The moves reflect Buffett's cautious repositioning amid shifting financial and consumer trends.

data.title: US Equity Indexes Rise in Midday Trading
data.description:
US equity indexes rose in midday trading Friday even after a consumer sentiment gauge slumped and inflation expectations surged.

The Nasdaq Composite advanced 0.2% to 19,142.1, the S&P 500 rose 0.2% to 5,929.2, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 0.2% to 42,412.3. Health care and utilities led the gainers, and technology and energy posted the only declines.

In economic news, the University of Michigan's preliminary consumer sentiment index fell to 50.8 in May from 52.2 in April, compared with expectations for an increase to 53.5 in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. According to a report from Trading Economics, the reading marks the fifth consecutive monthly decline, the lowest since June 2022 and the second-weakest on record.

Respondents pegged one-year inflation expectations at 7.3%, up from 6.5% in April, while five-year inflation expectations rose to 4.6% from 4.4%.

The survey period closed on May 13, two days after the pause on tariffs on Chinese imports.

Most US Treasury yields fell with the 10-year down 2.1 basis points to 4.43%.

Gold futures dropped 1.5% to $3,205.13 per ounce.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures rose 0.4% to $61.89 a barrel.

data.title: Carney Has Know-How to Secure Closer Canada-Europe Economic Ties — Market Talk
data.description: The head of one of Europe's biggest business-lobby groups believes Canada Prime Minister Mark Carney can--and needs to--deliver on increased economic and security integration with the continent. Fredrik Persson, president of BusinessEurope, tells WSJ that Canada and Europe have a decent launching pad to build on with the bilateral free-trade pact, known as CETA. "But it can be taken so much further," says Persson, on the sidelines of a B7 conference, involving corporate leaders from the G7 economies. He says increased trade with Europe won't totally offset the damage from US tariffs but would improve the outlook. Persson adds Carney has the personal networks to clinch closer economic networks. "He knows both sides of the Atlantic," Persson says, noting how he was governor at the Canadian and British central banks. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: AKM Law Study Warns of Alarming Rise in Immigration Fraud Targeting Newcomers to Canada
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario, May 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — A newly released study by AKM Law reveals a disturbing increase in immigration fraud cases across Canada, putting vulnerable newcomers at significant risk. The findings highlight the urgent need for public awareness, government vigilance, and proper legal guidance to protect immigrants from financial loss and legal jeopardy.

Immigration Fraud Cases Investigated Every Month

As Canada remains a top destination for global immigration, the surge in fraudulent immigration services and scams has grown in parallel. Unscrupulous actors are exploiting the hopes and trust of individuals and families seeking a better future, often leaving them financially drained and legally compromised. According to AKM Law, this trend is not only harming victims directly—it also undermines public trust in the Canadian immigration system.

Key Findings and Trends

The study from AKM Law outlines the most common types of immigration fraud currently affecting individuals across the country:

  • Fake Job Offers and Employment Scams: Fraudsters offer non-existent jobs in Canada to lure victims into paying fees or providing personal information.
  • Ghost Consultants: Unauthorized agents pretending to be immigration consultants or lawyers take money without providing any legitimate services.
  • Misrepresentation on Applications: Some victims unknowingly submit false information due to poor or misleading advice, risking serious legal consequences, including deportation.
  • Phishing and Impersonation Scams: Scammers often impersonate government officials, demanding fake payments for immigration processes.

These fraudulent activities disproportionately affect individuals with limited understanding of Canada’s immigration system and those who are unfamiliar with their legal rights.

A Call for Awareness and Action

“This is a deeply personal issue for many of our clients,” said a representative from AKM Law. “People come to Canada with dreams, and fraudsters are turning those dreams into nightmares. It’s essential for newcomers to work only with licensed immigration professionals and remain vigilant against red flags.”

The study encourages the Canadian government to continue enhancing consumer protection measures, including better regulation of immigration consultants, improved public education campaigns, and stricter enforcement against illegal actors. It also urges community organizations to play a proactive role in educating immigrants about their rights and safe practices.

Helping Newcomers Stay Informed and Protected

AKM Law emphasizes that individuals should always verify the credentials of immigration representatives and be wary of anyone promising guaranteed results or asking for large upfront fees. Understanding your rights and seeking guidance from qualified professionals can be the key to a successful and fraud-free immigration journey.

To read the full study, visit: Immigration Fraud: A Growing Concern and How to Avoid It

Types Of Immigration Fraud

About AKM Law

AKM Law is a Toronto-based immigration law firm dedicated to helping individuals and families navigate Canada’s immigration system with confidence. With a focus on transparency, integrity, and results, AKM Law provides comprehensive legal support for all immigration matters—including permanent residency, temporary visas, sponsorships, and appeals.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1132 ET - The big selloff in Doximity shares tied to its conservative outlook feels draconian and misses the bigger picture on the strides Doximity made during fiscal 2025, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The guidance feels appropriately de-risked given uncertainty in the wider economy, there have been no emerging signs of weakness in its market and backlog growth entering the year appears to be up a healthy 20%, the analysts say. The company seems to have made progress in fiscal 2025 on its client portal, new products and multi-module buying, which have the analysts more confident about the company's growth rate. The analysts maintain an outperform rating. Shares are down 14% at $50.16. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Doximity's fiscal 2025, which ended March 31, was a strong growth year that fiscal 2026 will have a tough time topping, Mizuho analysts say in a research note. Sales rose 20% from strong revenue retention, particularly among Doximity's top 20 customers. The provider of a networking platform for healthcare professionals now has 116 customers contributing more than $500 million in annual subscription revenue, and those customers account for 84% of FY25's revenue, lowering the risk of small-customer churn, the analysts say. Doximity also grew revenue by 2 to 3 percentage points by shifting to more multi-module integrated offerings, they say. That tough comparison with FY25, along with rising macro uncertainty, explains why Doximity is only guiding for half as much revenue growth in FY26, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1057 ET - Applied Materials' results and guidance were in-line, but JPMorgan is scaling back estimates. "We are lowering our FY/CY25 revenue/EPS estimates as we account for a weaker 2H demand profile driven by tariff/trade related dynamics for the overall semi/semicap industry," say analysts Harlan Sur and Peter Peng. The semiconductor-equipment company suggested it's not seeing any pull-forward of demand from future quarters due to tariff-led/trade dynamics nor have customers forecasts changed meaningfully, they add. Applied Materials slides 7%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1051 ET - There will be a debate around how conservative Doximity's guidance is until the company's typical upselling season kicks off in August, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. At that point, Doximity will have better clarity into how the budgets of its pharmaceutical clients are shaping up, the analysts say. As of right now, the company's outlook assumes some weakness in cross-selling and upselling, they say. But management says it hasn't seen any indications yet of its market slowing, and that Doximity faces less risk than it used to now that just under 70% of the year's revenue is already under contract, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1042 ET - Doximity hasn't seen any signs of its market slowing yet, but is giving conservative guidance on the year ahead in case shifting trade policies weaken the wider economy. "There is a big cloud of policy uncertainty that I think we're all assuming will continue this year," CEO Jeff Tangney says on a call with analysts. The telehealth company faced hard times coming out of the pandemic as doctors went back to the office and the economy stuttered. It learned from that experience that upselling to its pharma clients can become more variable when the macro visibility weakens, CFO Anna Bryson says on the call. Doximity is therefore guiding for its clients' budget growth to be on the lower side of its previous outlook, Bryson says. Doximity slides 15% on the soft outlook. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1020 ET - Applied Materials has good visibility and confidence on its 2030 roadmap despite near-term growth being mixed, say KeyBanc Capital Markets analysts. The semiconductor-equipment company issued 2Q EPS above estimates and forecast in-line revenue for 3Q. The analysts think Applied Materials took a relatively conservative tone toward near-term conditions but expressed confidence to deep customer relationships and take share on important node transitions toward $1 trillion to $1.3 trillion by 2030. "For now, the market continues to be driven by AI-related logic-and-memory devices, while trailing edge and China remain challenging," they add. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0941 ET - Applied Materials sinks 6% in early trading. Raymond James says the semiconductor-equipment maker's F2Q revenue and F3Q outlook were largely in-line with consensus while EPS is trending better on margin strength. "Lack of revenue upside is somewhat surprising given the recent results from peers," analyst Srini Pajjuri adds, which he attributes to the company's Internet of Things, Communications, Automotive, Power and Sensors [ICAPS] exposure. But Pajjuri says demand remains healthy in other markets as generative AI continues to drive spending. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0905 ET - Take-Two Interactive Software delivered a strong finish to fiscal 2025 thanks to broad based performance across core franchises and mobile, says Benchmark's Mike Hickey in a research note. The analyst says interest in the latest installment in the Grand Theft Auto franchise is building, too, "reinforcing the franchise's global cultural resonance and the anticipated impact of its fiscal 2027 release." The video game developer earlier this month delayed the launch of "Grand Theft Auto VI" to May 26 of next year, rather than this coming fall as previously announced. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0847 ET - Apple, having managed much of its supply chain disruption from the tariff war, can now focus on unveiling an AI strategy roadmap that will lead to "a renaissance of growth," Wedbush analysts say in a research note. The tech giant is expected to announce Alibaba as its AI partner in China prior to Apple's developer's conference in June, the analysts say. "This would lay the groundwork for a global AI strategy to take place over the next year with China a key piece of the growth puzzle," the analysts say. At the conference, investors should hear more about Apple's planned features and rollout of more Apple Intelligence, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0833 ET - Coinbase Global is facing a challenge or two but nothing that should substantially impact its position as one of the primary beneficiaries of institutional adoption of crypto, Benchmark's Mark Palmer says in a research note. The U.S. cryptocurrency exchange Thursday disclosed a significant cybersecurity breach and confirmed an ongoing SEC investigation regarding its past user metrics. This stands in contrast to news earlier this week that it was set to join the S&P 500. Palmer believes the cyberattack to be more of a one-off event and that the SEC's inquiry is "little more than noise that is highly unlikely to have any material impact on any of the drivers of the bullish thesis on the company's stock." Coinbase up 3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0822 ET - Apple has adopted a very hedged supply chain strategy with iPhone 17 production this fall and could ramp assembly production in India up to 60% to 65%, though it may pivot back to China depending on how the tariff fight plays out, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. The push toward India production has been a smart strategic move given the uncertain tariff environment around China, the analysts say. The Trump administration will probably keep putting pressure on Apple to build iPhones in the U.S., but that would hike iPhone prices up to a non-starter range of about $3,500, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0528 ET - NetEase's outlook remains positive, thanks to its strong game development ability, according to Citi analyst Alicia Yap in a research note. Upcoming title "Ananta" had received positive feedback on its first real-player testing earlier this year, Citi points out, citing management's remarks. The analyst remains positive on the company's strength in high-quality game development capability, its diversified genres and growing size of game portfolio with steady grossing and profit stream. Strong cash flow also generating ability and consistent shareholder returns are also positive factors for the company. Citi maintains a buy rating and keeps the target price of its ADRs at US$156.00. Its ADRs were last traded US$122.76. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0926 ET - Novo Nordisk's CEO departure comes as a surprise despite the steep share price decline over the past year, traders in Frankfurt say. Although the share's decline has been steep, the valuation nearly quintupled in the time leading up to it. "The CEO can't do anything about competition entering a market with (product) profitability like that," a trader says. The Danish pharma company--known for its weight-loss treatments--said it is currently seeking a successor for the CEO post. Shares trade 1.9% lower at 425.75 Danish kroner, after falling more than 4% following the news. (michael.denzin@wsj.com)

0338 ET - In the absence of a U.S. recession, investors are upbeat about corporate earnings growth, Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets says in a note. "While company executives continue to emphasize growth risks, they are simultaneously presenting robust balance sheets to their shareholders," he says. Companies from sectors outside of automobiles, pharmaceuticals and energy--which are worst hit by tariffs--are navigating the trade policy challenges quite successfully. There, business is "thiving" and investors are leaning toward these sectors, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

2124 ET - Guidance will be a focus of Oceania Healthcare's FY 2025 result on Thursday, says Forsyth Barr. "Oceania Healthcare has failed to deliver operationally and financially for several years, and the market is losing faith in its care-focused business model," says analyst Aaron Ibbotson. It identifies three areas that Oceania Healthcare must deliver on in FY 2026 to regain confidence. Head-office costs must fall meaningfully. Net debt must reduce significantly. And Oceania Healthcare must have in place a plan to sell down its flagship Auckland village, The Helier. "We expect clear communication on all three issues and retain Outperform," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1001 ET - Despite expectations that earnings for Canada's big banks have stalled, several are set to lift payouts to shareholders, BMO Capital Markets' Sohrab Movahedi says. The earnings season for banks kicks off May 22 starting with Toronto-Dominion Bank. Movahedi reckons the Big 5 banks, excluding Bank of Montreal, will turn in flat cash operating EPS following three consecutive quarters of double-digit year-on-year growth. The analyst expects credit quality and reserve adequacy to be a key focus this quarter amid macroeconomic uncertainty. Still, dividend increases are expected from Bank of Nova Scotia, Royal Bank and National Bank of Canada this quarter, Movahedi says. BMO has outperform calls on Royal Bank, TD Bank and Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce for their defensive qualities. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0833 ET - Coinbase Global is facing a challenge or two but nothing that should substantially impact its position as one of the primary beneficiaries of institutional adoption of crypto, Benchmark's Mark Palmer says in a research note. The U.S. cryptocurrency exchange Thursday disclosed a significant cybersecurity breach and confirmed an ongoing SEC investigation regarding its past user metrics. This stands in contrast to news earlier this week that it was set to join the S&P 500. Palmer believes the cyberattack to be more of a one-off event and that the SEC's inquiry is "little more than noise that is highly unlikely to have any material impact on any of the drivers of the bullish thesis on the company's stock." Coinbase up 3% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0514 ET - Barclays's current valuation in terms of price to earnings and price to tangible net asset value doesn't reflect its true worth and makes the bank the cheapest in Europe, J.P. Morgan Cazenove says in a research note. This seems to be largely due to the negative investment sentiment toward the U.S., where Barclays operates its investment banking and consumer businesses which bring in a third of group revenue, analysts Kian Abouhossein and Sheel Shah write. This view is incorrect as investment banking is the best growth segment thanks to expected high levels of volatility, they say. The U.S. credit card business could face some difficulties but it is a relatively small contributor to earnings, they add. Shares trade 1% higher at 325 pence and have risen 21% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0450 ET - Investors drew the wrong conclusions from Hannover Re's first-quarter print, Jefferies says in a research note following share weakness earlier this week. This wasn't a low-quality beat with currency gains offsetting softer underwriting, Philip Kett writes. Instead, the German reinsurer "opts to delay the emergence of one-off gains in earnings, trading it for balance sheet prudence that can smooth volatility later," he adds. This is a strategy management has used before, Kett adds. The long-term consequences of this delayed earnings recognition pattern is the accumulation of resilience reserves above the required levels, he adds. Shares recoup recent losses and trade up 1.7% at 280.8 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0427 ET - The removal of the ring-fencing regime for U.K. banks could save around 1.5 billion pounds for the sector by scrapping operational, funding and other costs, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. The CEOs of some of the country's top lenders wrote to the government asking it to abolish this regime, which requires banks with a certain level of core deposits to legally separate their retail banking activities from other operations. "We estimate that circa 160 billion pounds of excess liquidity is currently sitting in U.K. ring-fenced banks," analysts write, noting that this high quantity is likely due to several factors such as years of quantitative easing and higher savings since the pandemic. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0421 ET - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank is in a good position within its geographical network, J.P. Morgan Cazenove says. The CIB has a strong leadership team that has refocused the unit on returns, operating leverage and risk-weighed asset optimization, JPM Cazenove says in a research note after attending a webinar with its management. "We came away constructive on growth opportunities including deeper wallet penetration with material income multipliers as clients utilize more products/markets, and targeted focus on network and financial institutional income," analysts write. The targets outlined for the unit broadly align with expectations. StanChart is well-placed as supply chains adjust, though its shares are exposed to volatility until tariff uncertainty clears, they add. Shares in London have risen 16% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0357 ET - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank seems to be maintaining the good trajectory it has seen in the past five years, which was helped by leadership actions but also the period of rising rates and global volatility, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says. "It is often difficult to test management assertions... but it is clear that this business has outperformed in recent years," analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write in a research note after a webinar with management. The business, which is key for the group as it brings in two-thirds of pretax profit, is betting on the deepening wallet share of large clients in a fragmented world for its next phase of growth. Shares in London have gained 16% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0350 ET - Aegon's update feels a little messy overall but it won't likely move consensus expectations due to the one-off items, J.P.Morgan says in a note after the Dutch life insurer's first-quarter results. Analysts note the operating capital generation miss, the guidance retieration and the new 200 million-euro buyback, though it remains unclear whether there will be scope for another program this year. "The shares continue to look highly inexpensive to us; however, we are not sure if this update today will help close the valuation gap with peers," analysts write. Shares fall 1.2% to 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0346 ET - Aegon's figures didn't particularly excite but its share buyback looks moderately supportive, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's first-quarter print. The group announced a 200 million euro buyback that will run until the end of 2025, on top of its current 150 million program, which will be completed next month. "We feel that consensus was more optimistic for the year (300 million-400 million euro range we think)," analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The run-rate of its holding cash position, which management is guiding for a normalized level of around 1 billion euros for the year, leaves some flexibility, he adds. Shares are down 1.1% at 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0303 ET - Allianz has made steady progress recently which is already reflected in its share price, HSBC says in a research note. The German insurer's stock is trading above its one- and three-year averages and at a 3% premium to the sector. "We view Allianz's business model favorably with its strong balance sheet, diversification by business line and geography, as well as its focus on organic growth, productivity improvements, and improving economic value creation under its 2024-27 business plan," analysts write. However, they cut the rating on the stock to hold from buy as property and casualty headwinds are moderating and consensus already sits at the top end of management's guided range. Shares have risen by about a third over the year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0240 ET - Swiss Re's first-quarter results were unusually benign when looking beyond the California wildfires, Jefferies says in a research note. "Although natural catastrophe losses cost Swiss Re's Property & Casualty Re business $570 million in 1Q 2025, far more than the seasonal budget of $363 million, this loss burden was still 20% below consensus, and thus a key driver behind the group beating consensus by 36%," analyst Philip Kett writes. Its life and health reinsurance claims also contributed to the better result. While insurance revenue across business lines looks somewhat light, there is little not to like about the print, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0238 ET - Swiss Re profits beat consensus comfortably but this was driven by a higher investment result and lighter-than-expected natural catastrophe losses, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says after the reinsurer's first-quarter results. The combined ratio of its property and casualty reinsurance division was higher than consensus, which could be explained by the nominal catastrophe losses being lower than expectations despite being higher than what was budgeted. "Similarly to the other reinsurers, we think the market may be disappointed by the April renewals," analyst Darius Satkauskas writes in a research note. He also notes that though the headline price rose by 1.5%, the loss cost assumptions rose 3.7%, which leads to a negative 2.3% net price change. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0838 ET - Oil futures are little changed, looking to post a second straight weekly gain on easing trade tensions that lifted prices early in the week. Back-and-forth on U.S.-Iran nuclear talks buffeted prices, as President Trump followed threats of "massive maximum pressure," against Tehran with expressions of optimism about reaching a deal, while Treasury sanctioned more firms involved in Iranian oil trade. Global crude stocks increased, including a 3.5 million barrel build in the U.S., OPEC held its demand growth estimate at 1.3 million barrels a day, while the IEA sees a looser balance in raising its supply forecast by more than demand. WTI and Brent are up 0.1% at $61.67 and $64.60 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0629 ET - Orsted's valuation reflects a worst-case scenario, in which it would be ordered to halt work on its U.S. offshore projects, which is unlikely, UBS analyst Mark Freshney writes. Shares have fallen 37% year-to-date, reflecting the risk of a halt order on its Revolution and Sunrise projects, as was the case at Equinor's Empire Wind. "We view a halt order on the U.S. offshore projects as less-likely, given there is a local equity partner in Revolution Wind, high state of completion and local content." Revolution offshore works are to be completed early 2026 and Sunrise early 2027, which also removes risk, he adds. UBS lowers its price target to 400 Danish kroner from 420 kroner, and keeps at buy. Shares rise 0.7% to 246.40 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0338 ET - In the absence of a U.S. recession, investors are upbeat about corporate earnings growth, Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets says in a note. "While company executives continue to emphasize growth risks, they are simultaneously presenting robust balance sheets to their shareholders," he says. Companies from sectors outside of automobiles, pharmaceuticals and energy--which are worst hit by tariffs--are navigating the trade policy challenges quite successfully. There, business is "thiving" and investors are leaning toward these sectors, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0145 ET - Manila Electric appears to lack any further rerating catalysts, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. Its share price has risen 18% year-to-date through May 14, thanks to recent announcements including franchise renewal, the analyst notes. Also, its share price looks a bit overvalued based on the current price-to-earnings and price-to-book-value ratios, the analyst says. Hence, the brokerage downgrades the stock's rating to hold from add. However, given SB Equities makes no adjustments to its current assumptions about the Philippine electric distribution utility, the brokerage maintains the target price at PHP562.80. Shares are 1.3% lower at PHP562.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1502 ET - Oil futures post back-to-back losses as President Trump expresses optimism about reaching a nuclear deal with Iran, and the IEA forecasts a looser supply and demand balance this year despite a slight uptick in its demand growth forecast. Citi analysts nudged up their 0-3 month point price view for Brent to $60 on the cutback in U.S. and China tariffs earlier this week, but cited the probability of a U.S.-Iran nuclear deal in keeping its 2Q and 3Q averages at $62 and $63 a barrel, respectively. "Though if talks fall through and the situation escalates, oil could move to $70+," they add in a note. WTI settles down 2.4% at $61.62 a barrel, and Brent falls 2.4% to $64.53 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1435 GMT - Several indicators support the view that the U.S. airline industry is in the midst of an earnings recession, Deutsche analysts say in a research note. The analysts estimate system passenger volumes and average fares are trending lower, resulting in lower top lines. Demand appears to be down the most in domestic markets among price-sensitive customers, they say. International travel is still strong, though some conflicts abroad are denting certain markets: demand to travel to India has been in a free fall amid regional hostilities and Delta and United extended the suspension of their services to Israel, the analysts note. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1149 GMT - German carmakers' first-quarter results were a drag on overall earnings growth for companies in the DAX blue-chip index, equity and cross-asset strategists at Deutsche Bank say in a research note. On aggregate, first-quarter earnings at DAX constituents fell 8% compared with the year-earlier period and exceeded estimates by 5%, Deutsche Bank says. However, the picture is more positive when excluding the auto sector--earnings ere up by 4% and beat forecasts by 8%, according to Deutsche Bank. For 2025 as a whole, the auto sector is expected to weigh on the DAX as well, as the consensus puts earnings growth at 4% when including autos and 11% excluding the sector, the strategists say. The DAX is up 0.5%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1045 GMT - Renk Group's top-line outlook is very strong, JPMorgan analysts David Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say. JPM upgrades its rating on the stock to overweight from neutral because the German defense group's performance in the two most recent quarters has improved after the resignation of the CFO and CEO, they say. "Given the extremely strong outlook for European defense spending, especially in Germany, and very strong recent orders for armored vehicles containing Renk's products, the future looks very bright for Renk, in our view," the analysts write in a note to investors. Shares are up 8.5% at 60.21 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1012 GMT - Stellantis CFO Doug Osterman has again reiterated the potential for a company recovery, although current North American dynamics and tariff uncertainty allow for little detail at this time, Citi analysts write. The comments come after Osterman spoke at a fireside chat, Citi says. It seems likely that first-half earnings will remain at low levels, but after that, Stellantis sees significant recovery potential, the bank adds. Tariff risks remain substantial, but the company also has opportunities to maximize U.S. production and profitability of new U.S. products. The 3.75% tariff exemption on parts also adds valuable time to localize supply chains. "Stellantis remains at deep value earnings and valuation levels." Citi retains its 9 euro target price and neutral rating. Shares rise 0.2% to 9.59 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - Cyclical stocks should outperform in the current environment and European basic resources, real estate and autos have room for a further rally, Citi equity strategists say in a research note. The strategists cite Glencore, BMW, Pandora, Schneider Electric and Vonovia, among others, as beaten-up stocks for which analysts are dialing up earnings forecasts. Markets have moved swiftly higher as trade tensions between the U.S. and China eased and the likelihood of a recession receded, suggesting earnings uncertainty is already past its peak, Citi says. Nevertheless, there are still some cyclical stocks left, the strategists say. The Stoxx Europe 600 index rises 0.6%, having gained 2.3% this week. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - Singapore Airlines' earnings momentum faces multiple headwinds, the DBS Group Research team writes in a note. While forward bookings are positive so far, pricing pressures are likely to persist, given the global uncertainty, they write. Cost inflation is expected to remain elevated. Scoot, its low-cost carrier, particularly has faced intense competition, they add. Cargo is likely to perform better-than-expected in the near-term, thanks to front-loading ahead of potential tariffs, they say. SIA faced significant margin contraction in 4Q given cost pressures from higher-than-expected ex-fuel expenditure, but lower fuel prices will likely provide some relief in FY26, they write. DBS has a hold rating on the stock with a target price of S$6.30. Shares are 0.15% higher at S$6.89. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0738 GMT - In the absence of a U.S. recession, investors are upbeat about corporate earnings growth, Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets says in a note. "While company executives continue to emphasize growth risks, they are simultaneously presenting robust balance sheets to their shareholders," he says. Companies from sectors outside of automobiles, pharmaceuticals and energy--which are worst hit by tariffs--are navigating the trade policy challenges quite successfully. There, business is "thiving" and investors are leaning toward these sectors, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0633 GMT - Airports of Thailand faces an earnings hit from likely reduced passenger flows, Nomura analysts say in a research report. Expectations of a tourism slowdown in Thailand prompt the brokerage to cut FY 2025 and FY 2026 passenger-count assumptions for the airport operator by 7.6% and 6.6%, respectively. Hence, Nomura's earnings forecasts for AOT are lowered by 46.8% for FY 2025 and 47% for FY 2026. However, the brokerage notes that the sharp fall in the airport operator's share price since news of a liquidity crunch at its concessionaire has fully priced in consensus earnings reductions. Nomura maintains a buy rating on the stock and lowers the target price to THB44.00 from THB78.00. Shares are 1.4% higher at THB35.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0515 GMT - Singapore Post may be profitable in FY 2026, but the focus is in monetization of its assets, Maybank Research's Jarick Seet says in a research report. Management will likely quicken its efforts to further reduce the size and scale of its loss-making postal network and sell some properties to unlock value for shareholders, the analyst says. Also, the company will probably engage with the Singapore authorities more actively to pursue the sale of SingPost Centre. The postal and e-commerce logistics services provider posted an underlying net loss of S$500,000 in 2H FY 2025, notes the brokerage, which trims the target price to S$0.74 from S$0.77 while maintaining the buy rating. Shares are 2.7% higher at S$0.575. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0511 GMT - Singapore Airlines' passenger yields appears to be holding up, Citi Research analysts say in a note. This provided a boost to its full-year core earnings which slightly beat Citi's estimates. Incremental de-escalation in tariff tensions will likely help passenger pricing improve, and the relatively high payout ratio will likely support the share price, they add. The high payout could indicate delays in spending given on-going supply chain tightness, or higher confidence that Air India will not require further injections in the short term, they add. A key risk would be softer-than-expected passenger demand, ticket and cargo pricing, they add. Citi retains a neutral rating with a target price of S$6.34. Shares are 0.4% lower at S$6.85. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0206 GMT - ComfortDelGro's earnings look relatively sheltered from tariff risks, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a research report. Management's lack of mention of tariffs underscores the defensiveness of the transport company's earnings, given its domestic exposure within the markets where it's present, the analyst says. Also, its recently acquired CMAC Group has secured a contract with a U.K. online travel agency, which would help to further drive growth in this segment, the analyst notes. OCBC raises the stock's fair value estimate to S$1.71 from S$1.67 to partly reflect a higher cost-of-equity assumption, while maintaining the buy rating. Shares are 1.3% higher at S$1.51. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2226 GMT - Appaloosa raised its Uber stake to 3.2 million shares from 1.5 million in the first quarter, while reducing its Lyft position to 9 million from 13.5 million. Elsewhere in Thursday's 13-F filings, Soros Fund Management reported that it reduced its Uber position by 265,667 shares, to 638,218. It also decreased holdings of another Uber competitor, DoorDash. Meanwhile, Maplelane Capital exited its Uber position. Uber surged in February as Bill Ackman disclosed a roughly $2.3 billion stake. Last week, Uber reported that first-quarter revenue rose 14%, to $11.5 billion. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE 100 Ends Week on Positive Note
data.description:
The FTSE 100 extended its gains on Friday, reaching levels last seen before President Donald Trump announced global trade tariffs in early April.

Traders reacted to Trump’s announcement that the US will set new tariff rates in the coming weeks, though his administration cannot negotiate trade deals with all countries simultaneously due to limited capacity.

Trump said, “I think we’re going to be very fair.

But it’s not possible to meet the number of people that want to see us,” during a meeting in the UAE. This follows recent US trade deals, including tariff relief with the UK and reductions agreed with China.

Leading gainers included British American Tobacco (+3.6%), B&M European Value (2.5%), and GSK (2.4%), while Anglo American (-3.6%), Fresnillo (-2%), Antofagasta (-1.9%), Melrose (-1.8%), and Glencore (-1.7%) were among the biggest decliners.

data.title: Dutch postpone offshore wind farm tenders due to low interest
data.description:
The Netherlands will postpone tenders for two offshore wind farms with a total capacity of 2 gigawatts (GW) due to a lack of interest from potential bidders, the Dutch government said on Friday.

The tenders had originally been planned for September. The government said it would now only open a tender for one location in the North Sea with a capacity of 1 GW then.

The Dutch Climate ministry said in March that interest for the three sites it wanted to tender was very low, after energy firms Eneco and Orsted had said they saw no viable business case without subsidies.

The government said it was still looking into ways to reintroduce subsidies in its tenders for offshore wind farms, as prospective bidders are backing out of the current "zero subsidy" model.

Climate minister Sophie Hermans said detailed plans for the future tenders would be presented by the third quarter, after having missed her earlier announced mid-April deadline.

The Netherlands last year pushed back plans to increase offshore wind capacity from 4.7 GW to 21 GW to the end of 2032 instead of 2030, citing costs, supply chain difficulties and "challenges in timely decision-making".

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1155 ET - A mix of rainfall in drought-stricken areas and dryness in areas that have gotten a large amount of precipitation look to support newly planted crops across the U.S., says ADM Investor Services in a note. The key weather event is a storm system hitting Nebraska on Sunday, providing water to areas that are developing a worsening drought. A better system should move through Sunday into early next week with a much better chance at more widespread showers across the region, which would include Nebraska," says the firm. CBOT grains are mixed in midday trade, with most-active corn down 0.8%, soybeans up 0.2%, and wheat down 1.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1135 ET - Industrial metal futures like aluminum and copper make progress recuperating losses that followed President Trump's 'Liberation Day' on April 2. Aluminum prices are now back to the level they were trading at directly before Trump announced his tariffs on countries including China, Vietnam, and a host of other nations. According to FactSet data, the LME continuous contract for aluminum has risen over 9% since finding a year-low of $2,277.42 per metric ton on April 9 - when Trump announced that these tariffs were paused for 90 days. U.S.-China negotiations have been supporting aluminum, but production continues to be focused in China — with most analysts doubting any major shift in global aluminum production, says Commerzbank in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1059 ET - The conclusion of a tour through wheat fields in the U.S. Plains results in higher projections for winter wheat yields, this even as drought conditions grow worse in some Central U.S. states. The tour, sponsored by the Kansas Wheat Quality Council, says that a yield of 53 bushels an acre is expected out of Kansas, which is 3 bushels an acre more than forecast by the USDA in its May WASDE report. But overall production is expected to be lower by roughly 6.5 million bushels than the USDA estimates — which may be a sign that wheat streak mosaic virus is having an effect on Kansas' output, says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. CBOT wheat is down 1.5% in morning trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1023 ET - Live cattle futures are up 0.1% after closing lower in the last three sessions. Analysts are unsure if the recent downturn in cattle is simply the market catching its breath--"something of an intermediate term top that could have been our last hurrah through the summer, or just another downside correction in an otherwise bull market?" says StoneX in a note. Lean hog futures are up 0.5% this morning. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0945 ET - CBOT soybeans futures turn positive to open the day. Although easing pressure on soyoil futures is supportive for soybeans, there are other more fundamental factors that may limit soybean gains. "Although easing tensions between Mainland China and the U.S. are expected to slightly boost Chinese demand for U.S. soybeans, U.S. soybeans remain less competitive compared to Brazilian soybeans due to the ongoing 10% tariff," says BMI in a note. "With anticipated abundant supplies from Brazil, the increase in demand resulting from the tariff reduction is likely to be minimal." Brazilian crop agency Conab reported a record-high soybean harvest this year. Soybeans are up 0.7%, while corn rises 0.6% and wheat nudges down 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0927 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower and heading for weekly losses as shoulder-season demand remains weak while inventories have been building at a rate of more than 100 billion cubic feet a week. "Natural gas prices are falling due to fundamental looseness," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. Henry Hub physical prices around $3.20 "never confirmed the move up in futures," he adds. Nymex natural gas is off 0.4% at $3.350/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0926 ET - CBOT soybean oil futures are down 2.4% pre-market, continuing the decline seen yesterday. Weakness comes as traders and analysts debate the possibility of reduced renewable volume obligation [RVO] provisions for biofuels--after testimony from EPA head Lee Zeldin stating that RVOs will be reviewed in coming months. The market was hoping for a more immediate decision to raise blending obligations for biofuels into petroleum fuels. "When the EPA news spooked the soyoil market, beans followed suit," says Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. "With bean oil sharply lower again this morning, beans will likely struggle to mount much of a recovery until oil can find a low." Soybeans slide 0.2% premarket. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0838 ET - Oil futures are little changed, looking to post a second straight weekly gain on easing trade tensions that lifted prices early in the week. Back-and-forth on U.S.-Iran nuclear talks buffeted prices, as President Trump followed threats of "massive maximum pressure," against Tehran with expressions of optimism about reaching a deal, while Treasury sanctioned more firms involved in Iranian oil trade. Global crude stocks increased, including a 3.5 million barrel build in the U.S., OPEC held its demand growth estimate at 1.3 million barrels a day, while the IEA sees a looser balance in raising its supply forecast by more than demand. WTI and Brent are up 0.1% at $61.67 and $64.60 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0620 ET - Palm oil ended lower, tracking weakness in the soybean oil market, according to David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Prices were hurt by the expectation of lower the share of biodiesel in fuel for 2026, affecting soybean oil demand and weighing investors' sentiment, Ng says. He sees support at MYR3,700 a ton and resistance at MYR3,900 a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery falls MYR44 to MYR3,812 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0416 ET - Base metal prices fall in early trade, with LME three-month copper down 1.1% to $9,495.50 a metric ton and aluminum sliding 1.3% to $2,467 a ton. Despite the dip, both contracts are poised to end the week in positive territory, supported by easing fears of a tariff-induced recession after the U.S. and China reached a temporary trade deal. Risks on the supply side are also providing support, according to market watchers. "The recent increase in copper prices is driven by supply shortage concerns amid front-loading activities in the U.S. as the prospect of U.S. copper tariffs looms, as well as diminishing stock levels in Mainland China," say analysts at BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0352 ET - Gold prices are on track for a weekly loss of nearly 4% as easing trade tensions between the U.S. and China weaken demand for safe-haven assets. In early trading, futures fall 0.3% to $3,216.80 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar trades 0.2% higher on the week, making bullion more expensive for holders of other currencies. "Prices have come down from the fresh record high of $3,500/oz amid the rollback of the U.S. and China's recently announced tariffs," analysts at BMI say. "Nevertheless, we continue to hold a bullish outlook." BMI forecasts prices in a range of between $3,000 and $3,400 a troy ounce in the second and third quarters, supported by U.S. policy uncertainty, geopolitical conflicts and inflation concerns. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0332 ET - Oil prices are headed for a modest weekly gain due to easing trade tensions between the U.S. and China, but concerns over OPEC+ supply and a potential Iran deal continue to weigh on sentiment. Brent crude and WTI are flat at $64.56 and $61.63 a barrel, respectively, after dropping more than 2% in the previous session on signs that the U.S. and Iran might be moving closer to a nuclear agreement. A deal could unlock additional Iranian supply at a time when the market is already bracing for a surplus. "It would allow Iran to increase oil output with more willing buyers for its oil," ING analysts say. "This could result in additional supply in the neighborhood of 400,000 b/d." Meanwhile, demand concerns linger, with the IEA forecasting slower consumption growth and increasing supplies for the rest of the year. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)
